title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a family of graphs closed under isomorphism. Is it true that $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity if and only if $\mathcal{H}$ contains a graph with an even number of edges? Equivalently: is it true that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges, $d_H(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity?
context_text: The authors define $d_H(n)$ as the maximum fraction of all graphs on $[n]$ forming an $H$-code (a family with no two members whose symmetric difference is a copy of $H$). If every member of $\mathcal{H}$ has an odd number of edges then $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \geq 1/2$, motivating the question of whether containing an even-edge graph is exactly the condition that forces $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \to 0$. The question is stated as the central open problem and noted in the concluding remarks to remain wide open.

title: Gowers Conjecture (graph-clique symmetric difference)
statement_text: Any family of a constant fraction of all graphs on $[n]$, where $n$ is sufficiently large, contains two graphs $G_1, G_2$ such that $G_2$ is a subgraph of $G_1$ and the symmetric difference of the two graphs (the set of all edges of $G_1$ that are not in $G_2$) forms a clique.
context_text: Gowers raised this conjecture in a 2009 blog post [9]. It is strictly stronger than the assertion that $d_{\mathcal{K}}(n) \to 0$ (where $\mathcal{K}$ is the family of all cliques), which is itself open. As noted in [9], the question can be viewed as the first unknown case of a polynomial version of the density Hales-Jewett Theorem.
paper_contribution: The paper discusses the conjecture in the concluding remarks, notes it remains open, and establishes the related lower bound $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ using constructions from Hunter and Mubayi (references [5], [11], [4]).
notes: Reference [9] is Gowers' 2009 blog post. The weaker assertion $d_{\mathcal{K}}(n) \to 0$ is also explicitly noted as open.

title: Informal question: is $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors single this out as an interesting special case of Question 1.1. The companion question of whether $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ is answered affirmatively within the paper by appealing to an edge-coloring construction of Hunter and Mubayi [5] (modifying [11] and [4]); the upper-bound direction $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ remains open.
notes: The lower bound $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ is established in the paper; only the question of whether $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ remains open.

title: Open problem: tighter bounds for linear graph-codes
statement_text: Establish tighter bounds for the maximum possible cardinality of a linear family of graphs on $[n]$ in which no symmetric difference is a copy of a fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges.
context_text: The paper notes that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges the maximum size of a linear $H$-code is $o\bigl(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\bigr)$, but the proof via Ramsey's Theorem yields very weak quantitative bounds. Theorem 1.6 gives a tight result for the family $\mathcal{K}$ of all cliques as a model for what such tight results could look like.

title: Open problem: binary vectors with cube-length forbidden symmetric difference
statement_text: What is the maximum possible cardinality of a collection of binary vectors whose coordinates are indexed by the elements of the ordered set $[n]$, where no symmetric difference of two distinct members of the collection forms an interval of length which is a cube of an integer?
context_text: This is posed as an illustrative example of extending graph-code questions to other combinatorial structures. The corresponding Cayley graph has $2^n$ vertices and is triangle-free by Fermat's Last Theorem for cubes; its independence number is $o(2^n)$ by the Furstenberg–Sárközy Theorem and its extensions.

title: Open problem: minimum edge-coloring with odd intersection property
statement_text: Determine or estimate the smallest number of colors in an edge coloring of $K_n$ in which every copy of a given graph $H$ (or every copy of any member of a prescribed family $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs) intersects at least one color class by an odd number of edges.
context_text: The authors propose this in the concluding remarks as a natural variant of classical Ramsey Theory questions arising from the coloring method used throughout the paper to produce lower bounds on independence numbers of Cayley graphs.
